subject: Chemistry
title: Willow Bark: Nature's Aspirin
description: A micro-lesson that makes Willow Bark: Nature's Aspirin usable.

hook.text: For 3,500 years, people chewed willow bark to relieve pain. The active ingredient became the world's most used drug.

buildup.text: Ancient Egyptians, Greeks, and Native Americans all used willow bark. Hippocrates prescribed it for fever in 400 BC.

discovery.text: In 1828, chemists extracted salicin from the bark. The body converts salicin into salicylic acid, which reduces inflammation.

twist.text: Salicylic acid destroys the stomach lining. Felix Hoffmann at Bayer modified it into acetylsalicylic acid—gentler but effective.

climax.text: Bayer named it Aspirin in 1899. Over 100 billion tablets are consumed yearly. It also prevents heart attacks and strokes.

punchline.text: Tree bark to tablet. A 3,500-year-old remedy became modern medicine.

quiz.question: Why was salicylic acid modified to create aspirin?
options: Salicylic acid damaged the stomach lining, so Bayer made a gentler version
Salicylic acid was too expensive to extract from willow bark
The natural compound lost potency when stored as a pill
